skill.name: backend-dev-guidelines
skill.description: Guides AI assistants in building Node.js, Express, and TypeScript backend services with layered architecture, validation, observability, configuration, and testing standards.
skill.summary: Apply production-oriented backend patterns for TypeScript microservices.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and critical indicators, but review found they are Markdown documentation examples rather than executable skill code. The skill is publishable with warning because some examples could influence generated code toward broad telemetry capture or weak development secrets if copied without review.

medium_findings.title: Telemetry Example May Capture Sensitive Request Data
medium_findings.description: The Sentry example enables local variable capture and includes request data, IP, query string, URL, and user fields. It scrubs authorization and cookie headers, but generated code based on this example may still send sensitive request bodies, query parameters, IP addresses, or local variables to a third party unless teams add stronger filtering.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is documentation, not active exfiltration. Confidence is high enough because the example explicitly enables broad Sentry integrations and only partially scrubs sensitive fields.

medium_findings.title: Configuration Examples Include Weak Development Secrets
medium_findings.description: Configuration guidance shows sample passwords, token placeholders, and a default development session secret. The same section warns not to commit secrets and recommends environment variables in production, so this is a generated-code safety concern rather than malicious behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The weak values are visible in documentation examples and could be copied. Confidence is moderated because the surrounding guidance also tells users not to commit secrets.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Markdown Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick detections are caused by Markdown code fences and inline examples. No evidence was found that the skill itself executes shell commands or asks the assistant to run arbitrary commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged locations are fenced Markdown examples. The repository contains Markdown guidance files and no executable script files were identified in the reviewed file list.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Path Alerts Are Documentation and Relative Imports
low_findings.description: Path traversal and filesystem alerts are mainly from TypeScript import examples, project path labels, and configuration file loading examples. They do not grant the skill runtime filesystem access by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is documentation snippets rather than active code. A real generated application should still validate config file paths if it adapts these examples.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Found No Override Attempt
low_findings.description: No evidence found of text that claims system authority, tells the auditor to skip review, or attempts to override marketplace security instructions. One negative example uses the word NEVER as normal coding guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found only ordinary best-practice language and negative examples. No suspicious authority or instruction-hijacking language was present in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Third-Party Error Telemetry Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The monitoring guidance demonstrates Sentry request and local variable collection. This pattern needs stronger redaction rules before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example is legitimate monitoring guidance, but it can expose sensitive data if copied without additional filtering.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Placeholder Secret Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The configuration documentation includes weak sample values such as a development secret and example passwords. These should be replaced before production deployment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The values are examples and the document warns against committing secrets, but copyable weak defaults remain a meaningful generated-code risk.

content.value_statement: Backend services often drift into inconsistent structure, weak validation, and uneven error handling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code clear standards for layered TypeScript microservices.
actual_capabilities: Defines layered backend architecture for routes, controllers, services, repositories, and database access.
Guides Express controller patterns using BaseController, async wrappers, and consistent response handling.
Documents Prisma repository patterns, transaction usage, query design, and service boundaries.
Provides Zod validation patterns for request bodies, route parameters, query strings, and error formatting.
Describes unified configuration, Sentry monitoring, async error handling, and Jest testing practices.
limitations: The skill provides guidance and examples, not a runnable backend framework.
Examples are oriented toward Node.js, Express, TypeScript, Prisma, Zod, Jest, and Sentry.
Teams must adapt naming, project paths, auth middleware, and configuration sources to their own repositories.
Security-sensitive examples should be reviewed before being copied into production systems.

use_cases.title: Standardize a New Service
use_cases.description: Create a TypeScript microservice with clear route, controller, service, repository, validation, and test boundaries.

use_cases.title: Refactor Route Logic
use_cases.description: Move business logic out of Express routes into testable services and repository classes.

use_cases.title: Review Backend Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Check changes for validation gaps, direct configuration access, missing Sentry capture, and weak test coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Check Backend Structure
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner audit of an existing feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this backend feature against the backend-dev-guidelines skill. Identify route, controller, service, repository, validation, configuration, monitoring, and testing gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Feature Skeleton
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a TypeScript Express feature using layered architecture. Include the route, controller responsibilities, service methods, repository methods, Zod validation, and required tests.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Unsafe Route Logic
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Express route into the required architecture. Keep behavior stable while moving business logic into services, database access into repositories, and validation into Zod schemas.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a production readiness review using BFRI. Evaluate architecture fit, data risk, operational risk, testability, configuration safety, observability, and rollback concerns.

output_examples.input: A route handler mixes request parsing, Prisma calls, business rules, and response formatting.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports that the route violates layering, proposes a controller and service split, adds repository methods, and lists focused tests.

output_examples.input: A new user-management feature needs validation, persistence, error handling, and monitoring.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes the route, controller, service, repository, schema, Sentry capture points, and unit plus integration test coverage.

output_examples.input: A pull request adds direct environment variable reads and unhandled async work.
output_examples.output: The assistant flags the configuration and async safety gaps, then recommends unifiedConfig access and explicit awaited error handling.

best_practices: Use the skill before implementing routes, controllers, services, repositories, middleware, or configuration changes.
Pair generated examples with repository-specific conventions before merging code.
Review security-sensitive configuration, monitoring, and auth examples before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder secrets, local URLs, or sample credentials into deployed systems.
Do not bypass the service and repository layers for quick database access from routes.
Do not treat the examples as complete application security controls without review.

faq.question: What stack does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets Node.js, Express, TypeScript, Prisma, Zod, Jest, and Sentry based backend microservices.

faq.question: Can this skill generate a full backend application?
faq.answer: It can guide structure and examples, but it is not a complete application scaffold or framework.

faq.question: Does it enforce a specific architecture?
faq.answer: Yes. It prefers routes, controllers, services, repositories, and database access as separate layers.

faq.question: Is it useful for code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives concrete checks for validation, configuration, observability, error handling, and testing.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It includes validation, configuration, and monitoring guidance, but production security review is still required.

faq.question: Can teams adapt the conventions?
faq.answer: Yes. Teams should adapt paths, middleware names, repository style, and deployment configuration to their codebase.
